MAY ISSUE · 2026
EXCLUSIVE LOOK INTO THE AGENCY — A SEASON IN MARKETING & LUX
A Season in Marketing & Lux
Ginevra Margaglia
The Agency, Beausoleil
Jan – June 2026
BBA3 · IUM Monaco
May 2026
Dr. Sargenti & D. Baron
Monaco · Spring 2026
Season Opening
DEAR
READER,

As a Marketing Intern and Pinterest obsessed, I couldn't resist putting my creative drive to work… which turned into creating a magazine telling the story of the last five crazy months of my Bachelor.

Imagine if Carrie Bradshaw had to tell the story of her life in Monaco in 2026, as a twenty-something trying to make sense of the beautiful (and scary) chaos of early adulthood.

Looking back, these past three years at IUM have taught me more than I could have imagined, both personally and professionally. From classroom lessons and business presentations, to soaking up the sun in the courtyard, coffee breaks, making friendships that I will treasure for life — every moment has shaped me.

Slowly but surely, I've been finding myself and my place. I don't know if Monaco will be my future, or where my career will take me, but my experience at The Agency felt like the perfect culmination of my journey at IUM.

See you at graduation ;)

Ginevra Margaglia
Editor-in-Chief
WHAT'S
/NSIDE
Vol. One — Spring 2026
    01Opening Column→ 02The Maison→ 03Day in My Life→ 04Agency Report→ 05The Season→ 06The Agency→ 07Collections→ 08The Craft→ 09Lessons→ 10Au Revoir→
01 — Executive Summary
Five months. Twelve luxury brands. Two departments. One full-time offer.

This report documents five months at The Agency — a boutique luxury digital marketing firm in Beausoleil — from January to June 2026. The placement was split across two disciplines: social media management for twelve luxury clients in yachting, private aviation, and prestige automotive; and digital marketing — website development, SEO, backlinking, landing pages, and Google Ads.

What follows is not just an academic report. It is a document of becoming — of understanding what luxury actually demands from those who communicate it, and what a season of professional immersion can do to a person's ambitions.

Social MediaGoogle AdsSEO / SEMLuxury BrandingWeb DevelopmentAI Integration
1,000+
Professional hours accumulated across five months
12
Luxury brand clients managed simultaneously across Instagram, LinkedIn & Facebook
5+
Websites built or significantly contributed to, from concept to live
72h
To launch luxabacus.com from scratch, in full, with a colleague
"In luxury, there are no small details — only details someone hasn't looked at closely enough."
02 — Introduction

The
Agency

A boutique digital marketing and communications firm specialising entirely in the luxury sector — a rare and deliberate focus that shapes every decision it makes, every hire it considers, and every caption it approves before it goes live on behalf of a superyacht brand.
Founded byNicolas Saugnac
HeadquartersBeausoleil, France
SpecialisationLuxury digital marketing
SectorsYachting · Aviation · Automotive · Real Estate
VenturesS-Collection · Lux Abacus · Jet Collection

The Agency operates as a full-service digital partner for clients in the world's most aspirational industries. Its services span social media strategy, website development, content production, SEM, and performance analytics — positioning it as a single point of accountability for brands that cannot afford inconsistency.

My role was split across two principal functions. First: social media management, where I assumed complete responsibility for twelve luxury brand clients across Instagram, LinkedIn, and Facebook — content planning, copywriting, visual briefing, community management, and monthly performance reporting. As trust and proficiency grew, I was given direct charge of client communication, including monthly debriefs and content approval cycles.

The second was digital marketing: website content creation, backlink article production, targeted landing page development, and the setup and optimisation of Google Ads campaigns across Search, Display, and Performance Max.

I also contributed to five websites from scratch — including luxabacus.com, launched in under 72 hours — and three S-Collection ski destination sites for Verbier, Megève, and Méribel.

After the third month, a full-time offer arrived. That was the real grade.

The Offer
"After three months, they offered me the job. The correct answer — when you're twenty-one and overlooking Monaco — is yes."
The Brief
A Jack-of-all-trades placement — strategic breadth over narrow depth. Exactly what was needed.
Port Hercule — The Agency's backyard
The rhythm of the Riviera
On Luxury
In luxury, every detail is the message — and the message is always about who you are not trying to reach.
A guiding principle, learned across twelve client accounts
03 — Macro-Environment

PESTEL
Analysis

The Agency does not operate in a vacuum. Six forces shape every campaign brief, every content calendar, every client relationship. Hover each card to explore the dynamic at play.

P
Political
Franco-Monégasque dual-jurisdiction stability provides a reassuring baseline. The EU Digital Services Act imposes growing advertising transparency requirements, directly shaping campaign architecture. Geopolitical instability in superyacht and private aviation sectors creates volatility that filters through to marketing budgets with notable speed.
E
Economic
The UHNWI market remains structurally resilient. Monaco's concentration of high-net-worth wealth — driven by financial services, real estate, and luxury tourism — creates a naturally fertile client environment. A diversified international client base across London, Dubai, and the US provides further structural resilience against market contractions.
S
Social
Millennial and Gen Z luxury consumers are the critical growth segment: digitally native, values-driven, and heavily influenced by social media brand presence. They expect authentic storytelling alongside aspiration. Growing scrutiny of high-emission sectors — yachting, aviation — creates pressure on brands to evolve their narratives without abandoning the dream.
T
Technological
AI integration across all workflows — Grok, Gemini, ChatGPT for ideation; Elementor and Divi for web; CapCut and Premiere for video — represents a genuine operational advantage. But technology is an equaliser as much as differentiator. Algorithm pivots on Instagram Reels and Google Performance Max require continuous, rapid adaptation from the entire team.
E
Environmental
The Agency's direct footprint is minimal. The indirect pressure is significant — clients in yachting and private aviation face intensifying sustainability scrutiny. Helping these brands communicate responsible narratives without straying into greenwashing is one of the most delicate and important challenges in luxury communications today.
L
Legal
GDPR governs every touchpoint where user data is processed — critical in performance marketing. Aviation and marine advertising operate under sector-specific standards. The IP status of AI-generated content is an emerging and unresolved frontier. International client contracts demand cross-jurisdictional legal vigilance that a small, agile team must navigate carefully.
04 — Company Analysis

SWOT
Analysis

Internal — Positive

Strengths

  • +Precise luxury sector focus — uncommon depth that generalists cannot credibly replicate
  • +Founder's personal authority: 100k+ followers, 500k monthly website visits
  • +French Riviera location — dense network of luxury clients, events, and opportunities
  • +Proactive AI integration across creative and technical workflows
  • +Full-service offering — one strategic partner, not a vendor
Internal — Negative

Weaknesses

  • −Lean team creates capacity pressure during high-volume periods and event seasons
  • −Concentrated luxury sector exposure introduces cyclical risk
  • −Boutique scale limits ability to compete on volume pricing against larger groups
  • −No formalised sustainability communications framework — a gap in an increasingly scrutinised space
  • −High dependency on Google and Meta creates structural platform vulnerability
External — Positive

Opportunities

  • ↑Expanding luxury digital marketing budgets globally — performance-led strategy increasingly valued
  • ↑Emerging UHNWI markets in the Middle East and Asia-Pacific
  • ↑S-Collection, Lux Abacus, and Jet Collection offer revenue diversification
  • ↑AI integration as efficiency differentiator versus less agile competitors
  • ↑Growing demand for curated, authentic social media presence across all luxury categories
External — Negative

Threats

  • !Larger digital groups building dedicated luxury practices — well-resourced competition entering the niche
  • !Algorithm changes on Meta and Google disrupting organic strategies without warning
  • !Luxury brands developing in-house marketing capabilities — reducing agency dependency
  • !AI content tools commoditising production services and compressing perceived value
  • !Privacy regulation tightening paid campaign targeting precision
"Reputation built through networks carries different weight than reputation built through reach. In luxury, discretion is itself a brand value."
From the branding analysis — The Agency, 2026
05 — Competitive Environment

Porter's
Five Forces

The structural dynamics of the luxury digital marketing industry — evaluated through the lens of Michael Porter's competitive framework. Each force is assessed for intensity and trajectory.

Competitive Rivalry
Moderate
A small number of credible boutique competitors in the Monaco–Riviera corridor. Rivalry is real but manageable. The founder's personal authority and sector depth are decisive differentiators. Competition is won on trust and cultural understanding, not pricing.
New Entrants
Moderate
Financial barriers to digital marketing are low. But establishing credible positioning within the luxury sector requires years of demonstrated track record. Authenticity and access are the real moats — both are difficult to purchase.
Buyer Power
High
Luxury marketing directors are sophisticated buyers with clear KPIs, multiple options, and data benchmarking capability. The Agency mitigates this through deep institutional brand knowledge that is genuinely difficult to replicate. Retention requires continuous strategic delivery.
Supplier Power
Low
Creative talent, software tools, and ad platforms are broadly available and not concentrated. The Agency enjoys high operational flexibility. Platform dependency on Google and Meta is the primary supplier risk — partially mitigated by a diversified technology stack.
Substitutes
Growing
AI tools are steadily reducing the cost of basic production. Luxury brands increasingly explore in-house capabilities. However, strategic counsel, cultural nuance, and high-stakes brand decisions remain deeply human competencies. The threat is to execution, not to strategy.
On sustainability
"Everything will fall into place."
06 — Sustainability & Ethics

Beyond
the brief

Yachting and private aviation are extraordinary industries. They are also among the highest-emitting leisure activities in existence. This creates a genuine challenge for any communications partner: how do you tell an aspirational story with honesty intact?

"The Agency plays a critical role in how its clients communicate their environmental positioning — and with that role comes a responsibility that extends well beyond the brief."

AI tools were used extensively throughout the internship — for content ideation, visual generation, and workflow automation. Their efficiency gains are significant. But so are the ethical questions: intellectual property ambiguity, the risk of homogenised content, and the potential for audiences to detect — and resent — the absence of genuine human craft.

GDPR compliance governed every decision in performance marketing. Targeted advertising depends on user data; user data requires informed consent. This wasn't abstract legal compliance — it shaped campaign architecture in practical, tangible ways every single week.

Environmental ImpactGreenwashing RiskAI Ethics & IPGDPR ComplianceDigital Carbon
07 — Branding Strategy

Selling
distinction

The Agency's brand strategy rests on a single, deliberate choice: niche over scale. Rather than competing as a generalist digital partner, it operates where its understanding of UHNWI consumer behaviour, luxury aesthetics, and premium brand dynamics creates genuine, defensible advantage.

This positioning allows the agency to build an identity on exclusivity, expertise, and a shared vocabulary with its clients. The communication style — refined visual aesthetics, aspirational storytelling, meticulous attention to detail — mirrors the core principles of the luxury brands it represents. There is no inconsistency between what The Agency produces for clients and how it presents itself to prospects.

A critical element of the strategy is the development of long-term client relationships. The agency positions itself not as a vendor, but as a strategic partner invested in shaping the digital identity of its clients over time. This relational approach creates retention dynamics that purely transactional agencies cannot replicate.

One observation worth noting: The Agency's own social media presence is notably modest relative to the sophistication of its client work. This may be intentional — in luxury, discretion is a brand value, and reputation built through private networks carries different weight than mass visibility. A selective, curated presence could strengthen inbound without compromising exclusivity.

Niche Positioning
Luxury-only focus creates depth that generalists cannot credibly replicate
Relational Model
Long-term partnerships over transactional engagements — strategic partner, not vendor
Geographic Authority
French Riviera and Monaco proximity as a source of authentic cultural credibility
Aesthetic Coherence
The agency's own communication mirrors luxury brand codes — refined, deliberate, aspirational
Strategic Recommendation
A selective social presence could enhance inbound credibility without sacrificing discretion
The Season
Villa Dolce Vita — this was the brief, the backdrop, and the education.
Five months on the French Riviera, learning what luxury truly demands
08 — Performance & Campaigns

Measuring
what matters

Google Ads Manager
Click-Through Rate
CTR
Monitored per campaign to evaluate ad copy quality and targeting relevance. Iterative copy testing used to improve performance — small language choices had measurable commercial consequences.
Google Ads Manager
Cost Per Click
CPC
Tracked alongside CTR to assess campaign efficiency. Bid strategy adjustments based on CPC trends against conversion targets — the balance between reach and cost is never static.
Google Ads / Analytics
Return on Ad Spend
ROAS
The primary commercial metric for all paid evaluation. Reported monthly to clients — the number that determines whether a campaign continues, scales, or is restructured entirely.
Google Analytics
Conversion Rate
CVR
Tracked for landing pages and campaign destinations. Conversion data informed iterative page design improvements — UX and performance marketing are inseparable.
Meta Business Suite
Social Engagement
E/R
Monthly reach, impressions, engagement rate, and follower growth tracked across all twelve accounts. Presented in client-facing documents — where data becomes storytelling.
Google Search Console
Backlink Performance
SEO
Backlink articles tracked for indexing and domain authority contribution. All written without AI tools — a constraint that sharpened the craft and deepened understanding of SEO principles.

Note: Specific numerical data withheld in accordance with client confidentiality commitments.

After three months, they offered me the job. The correct response — when you are twenty-one and standing in a Beausoleil office overlooking Monaco — is to say yes, and figure it out.
Ginevra Margaglia — On receiving the full-time offer
09 — Role Reflection & Growth

What the
season taught me

12 cl.
Managed simultaneously across three platforms
5 sites
Built or co-developed from concept to live launch
72h
To launch luxabacus.com from scratch
1 offer
Social Media & Marketing Manager — after month three

Managing social media for luxury clients required something I had not fully anticipated: the rapid internalisation of twelve completely distinct brand identities. Not just tone of voice, but a deep understanding of what each brand feared — a caption too casual, an image too generic, a response too slow. In luxury, the margin for brand inconsistency is extremely narrow.

The technical dimension was equally demanding. I developed working proficiency in Google Ads Manager — keyword research, bid strategy, ad copy testing, campaign optimisation — and built a solid understanding of the analytical frameworks that separate performance marketing from creative guesswork.

On the Tools

Asana for task management, Later for scheduling, Figma for visual briefing, Adobe Photoshop and InDesign for assets. I also built customised GPT tools calibrated to each client's specific tone of voice — accelerating production without sacrificing brand coherence. WordPress with Elementor and Divi Builder became genuinely familiar territory through five website projects.

On the Culture

The Agency operates with a flat, collaborative structure where ownership is expected rather than encouraged. My supervisor's management style — results-oriented, with independence in method — was precisely what accelerated my development fastest. The psychological safety to ask questions, propose ideas, and occasionally fail without consequence is not something all organisations offer interns. I was fortunate.

And then there is Victoria — my colleague, companion, and now dear friend. From shared lunches in the sun to ice creams in the kitchen and coffee breaks between deadlines, she made the intensity of the experience feel lighter. These are the parts of a career that no framework captures, but that matter most.

Key tools mastered
Asana · Later · Figma · Adobe PS · InDesign · CapCut · Premiere · Google Ads Manager · WordPress · Elementor · Divi
AI tools integrated
Grok · Gemini · ChatGPT — deployed for content ideation, visual generation & workflow automation
The lesson
In luxury, the smallest detail carries the greatest weight. A single misplaced word can undermine months of brand-building.
Slowly but surely, I've been finding myself — and my place.
Beausoleil → Monaco → Wherever comes next
End of analysis — Beginning of what comes next

What
comes next

This internship at The Agency has been a formative experience in the most literal sense — it has formed things. New competencies. New instincts. A sharper eye for brand coherence, a deeper respect for craft in communication, and a clearer sense of where luxury brand management and digital strategy intersect in ways that genuinely interest me.

The dual exposure to social media strategy and performance marketing was unusual — most internships offer one or the other. The breadth was demanding and, in retrospect, exactly what was needed. Theory encountered practice every single day, across twelve client accounts and five website launches.

The season was good. The next one will be better.

Immediate intentions
01
Accept the full-time offer as Social Media & Marketing Manager
02
Deepen analytics and Google Ads campaign architecture skills
03
Explore Master's programmes in luxury management — IUM or abroad
04
Integrate this experience with a direction toward luxury event management
05
Continue building Lore — where the personal and professional converge
